categoryDescription: Looks at the ETF's past performance: multi-year returns, recent momentum, calendar-year consistency, AUM trend, and standing inside its category peer group. Five factors apply to every ETF group. The `factorDescription` says what is measured and gives the generic Pass/Fail rule. `groupInstructions` gives the group-specific perspective — which benchmark to use, when technicals matter, distribution / NAV-erosion checks, futures roll cost, daily-leverage decay. When the two seem to conflict, follow `groupInstructions`.

factors.factorDescription: Looks at multi-year compound growth (5 / 10 / 15 / 20Y CAGR and trailing returns) and asks whether the fund beat the group's benchmark over each long window. This is the mandate test for any style premium, theme, leveraged target, or strategy promise — did it actually deliver? For funds younger than 5 years, use only the periods you have and note the short history. Pass when CAGR matches or beats the benchmark across most windows, or stays within tracking tolerance for a passive index fund. Fail when CAGR trails the benchmark across several long windows without a mandate-based reason.
factors.factorMetrics: cagr5y, cagr10y, cagr15y, cagr20y, return5y, return10y, returnsAnnual, returnsTrailing, indexName

groupInstructions.broad-equity: Compare CAGR to the index the fund tracks: S&P 500 for US large blend, MSCI EAFE for foreign developed, MSCI ACWI for global, Russell 1000 Growth/Value for US style tilts. For style-tilted funds (value, growth, dividend, low-vol, quality), the tilt should beat the parent index over long windows when its style is in favor. Plain Large Blend / Total Market should sit within tracking tolerance of the S&P 500.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Compare CAGR to both the sector / theme index AND the S&P 500. The S&P 500 check is the mandate test — a theme that just tracked the broad market over 10Y has not delivered on its thesis. For single-country EM, use the country index (MSCI China, Nifty 50, MSCI Brazil) plus MSCI Emerging Markets as the diversified-EM reference.
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Long-horizon CAGR is the daily-reset decay test. Quote the underlying's CAGR × stated leverage as the textbook expectation, then show the actual result and call the gap compounding decay. State plainly: these are short-term trading vehicles, never buy-and-hold. The 'how much would $10k be today' question is the wrong frame here. Do not compare directly to the S&P 500.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Compare CAGR to the spot benchmark (LBMA Gold, COMEX silver, Bloomberg Commodity Index, CME CF BTC / ETH Reference Rate) AND to the S&P 500. For futures-based commodity wrappers, the gap between spot and fund is NAV erosion from contango / roll cost — call it out (USO lost ~94% of NAV from inception due to roll cost). For physical gold / silver and spot crypto, note tracking efficiency vs spot (custody / management drag).
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Long-term CAGR is the mandate test. Covered-call funds (JEPI, QYLD, SPYI) should deliver yield + capped upside + cushion in down markets — verify all three. Long-short / market-neutral should show equity-like-or-lower CAGR with materially lower drawdown. Managed-futures should show crisis alpha in 2008 / 2020 / 2022. Compare TOTAL return (with distributions) to both the underlying equity index and a high-dividend equity ETF. Flag a flat or negative 10Y price return paired with a positive total return — that means distributions are partly return-of-capital.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Compare CAGR to a same-period 60/40 (S&P 500 + Bloomberg US Aggregate) and to the allocation peer-category median. Also compare to the DIY equivalent the reader could build (e.g. 80% VTI + 20% BND for aggressive, 30% VTI + 70% AGG for conservative) — the fund's value-add is automatic rebalancing, not alpha. Mandate-band check: moderate ~5-7%, conservative ~4-5%, aggressive ~7-9%. Target-date funds should track the issuer's published glide path. For tactical / global allocation, ask whether active calls beat a passive 60/40 over multi-year windows.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Compare CAGR to the Bloomberg US Aggregate (broad core) or a duration-matched Treasury index (ICE BofA 1-3Y / 7-10Y / 20+Y Treasury). For muni funds, compare to the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index and quote tax-equivalent CAGR at ~32% federal (plus state exemption for in-state holders). For TIPS, use a TIPS index and separate real vs nominal returns. If the fund's yield is below cash / HYSA, say the reason to hold it is optionality (price rally if rates fall), not yield.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: Compare CAGR to the right credit benchmark — Bloomberg US Corporate High Yield, S&P/LSTA Leveraged Loan, ICE BofA US Preferred Stock, JPM EMBI Global Diversified, or ICE BofA US Convertible. Also quote a same-period 60/40 CAGR — retail's honest question is whether they were paid for taking real default and subordination risk. Translate 'high yield' plainly: below-investment-grade credit with real default risk.

factors.factorDescription: Looks at recent returns over 1M / 3M / 6M / YTD / 1Y. Separate short-term momentum (1M, 3M) from trailing performance (6M, 1Y) — a strong 1Y with weak recent months is usually a normal pullback, not broad weakness. For three groups where entry timing matters (sector / thematic, leveraged / inverse, commodities / digital), also read the technical signals (MA20 / 50 / 150 / 200, RSI daily / weekly / monthly, distance from 52w high / low and ATH / ATL). For the other five groups, MA / RSI signals are noise — keep technicals brief. Every short-term number needs the same-period number for the group's benchmark. Pass when short-term performance matches or beats the benchmark and the trend is decision-useful for the fund's typical holding horizon. Fail when materially lagging across multiple recent windows without a mandate reason.
factors.factorMetrics: return1m, return3m, return6m, returnYtd, return1y, returnsAnnual, ma20, ma50, ma150, ma200, ma50ChgPercent, ma200ChgPercent, rsi, rsiW, rsiM, high52wChg, low52wChg, athChgPercent, atlChgPercent

groupInstructions.broad-equity: Compare 1M / 3M / 6M / YTD / 1Y to the tracked index (S&P 500, MSCI EAFE, MSCI ACWI, Russell 1000 style index). Flag whether near-term weakness is fund-specific (lagging the index) or a broad-market move that hit every peer. MA / RSI signals are usually noise for buy-and-hold broad-equity — keep technical commentary brief unless price is at a clear extreme (monthly RSI >70 or <30).
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Compare to both the sector / theme index AND the S&P 500 over each window — retail wants to see whether the sector bet is currently beating or fading vs the broad market. Sector cycles drive forward returns: read MA50 / MA200 position and RSI to gauge entry timing. Flag overbought (monthly RSI >70) or oversold (<30) and tie to the sector's macro cycle (rate-sensitive sectors near a rate pivot, energy near supply news, etc.).
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Short-term return IS the decision frame. Compare 1M / 3M / 6M / YTD / 1Y to the stated daily-leverage multiple of the unleveraged underlying — a 3x fund should be roughly 3x the underlying's same-period move minus reset slippage. Flag any large gap as path-dependency loss. The honest comparison is 'vs not holding this at all' — most retail investors should not hold these beyond a few trading days. Read price vs MA20 / 50 / 150 / 200 and RSI (monthly RSI >75 is stretched even by trading standards). Frame current entry vs the 52-week range.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Compare to the spot underlying (LBMA Gold, BCOM, CME CF BTC / ETH Reference Rate) over each window. For futures-based commodity wrappers, show the spot vs fund gap and call out roll cost (contango / backwardation). These are momentum-driven asset classes — quote MA50 / MA200 position and RSI (monthly RSI >70 stretched, <30 washout) and frame current price vs ATH / 52-week range.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Compare 1M / 3M / 6M / YTD / 1Y TOTAL return to the underlying equity index the strategy writes options on (S&P 500 for JEPI / SPYI, Nasdaq-100 for QYLD / XYLD / QQQI). Distribution composition matters — option-premium income, ordinary dividends, short-term capital gains, return of capital. Headline yield alone misleads; flag whether ROC is propping the yield while NAV erodes. MA / RSI is noise — keep technical commentary minimal.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Compare 1M / 3M / 6M / YTD / 1Y to a same-period 60/40 (S&P 500 + Bloomberg US Aggregate) and to the allocation peer-category median. For target-date funds, also compare to the issuer's published peer group for the dated year. MA / RSI signals are noise for allocation funds — keep technical commentary minimal.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Compare 1M / 3M / 6M / YTD / 1Y to a duration-matched Treasury or core-bond index. For muni, compare to a national muni index of similar duration. Flag whether near-term moves are rate-driven (parallel with peers) or fund-specific (active calls, tracking drift). Distribution should track SEC yield — large divergence signals smoothing. MA / RSI is noise here.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: Compare 1M / 3M / 6M / YTD / 1Y to the matching credit benchmark (Bloomberg US Corporate High Yield, S&P/LSTA Leveraged Loan, ICE BofA US Preferred Stock, JPM EMBI Global Diversified, ICE BofA US Convertible). Flag whether short-term weakness is spread-widening that hit the whole sub-asset class or fund-specific. Distribution moves with reference rates (SOFR-linked coupons reset quarterly) and default cycles.

factors.factorDescription: Looks at how stable returns have been across periods and calendar years. Quote calendar-year hit rate (how often the fund had a positive year), worst single year, and percentile-rank trend year-over-year (cite the actual movement, e.g. `14 → 87 → 18`, not just 'volatile'). Benchmark-matched bad year rule: a passive fund whose worst year is in line with its index and category is not a Fail — that is the asset class moving, not fund failure. For income-paying funds, also check distribution stability: did the headline yield hold up, ratchet down, or get propped up by return-of-capital? A flat total return on top of a steadily eroding NAV is not real consistency. Pass when the calendar-year pattern fits the group's typical dispersion and distributions (where relevant) held up. Fail when the fund swings materially harder than the group benchmark, OR distributions were cut sharply, OR total return is being propped up by ROC.
factors.factorMetrics: returnsAnnual, return1y, return3y, return5y, return10y, percentileRanks, dividendTtm, divGrowth3y, divGrowth5y, divYears, divGrYears, dividendYield, overviewSecYield, change1y, change5y, change10y

groupInstructions.broad-equity: Show calendar-year hit rate, worst single year, and percentile-rank trend against the S&P 500 / tracked index. A 5-year migration up or down the percentile ladder matters more than any single year. For broad-equity income / dividend tilts (High Dividend Yield, Dividend Aristocrats-style), also check whether the distribution held up year-over-year.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Sector and country returns swing harder than the broad market. Quote the worst single year — sectors regularly drop 30-50% in their bad year; single-country EM can drop 40-50% — and whether that lined up with broad-market bad years or was sector-specific. Quote the same-period S&P 500 pattern for contrast.
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Consistency is structurally poor here. Quote calendar-year wins vs losses, the worst single year (-50% to -90% common in adverse years), and recovery duration. Retail needs to see plainly that consistency is not a design feature of these products — reinforce the short-term-only warning every iteration.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Calendar-year dispersion is extreme: gold has +25-30% years inside multi-year flat stretches; broad commodities swing ±20-30%; BTC routinely ±50-100% in a single year. Compare hit rate vs the S&P 500 over the same window so retail sees the trade-off. For futures-based wrappers, a steadily declining 5Y / 10Y price with positive distribution-yielded total return is roll-cost NAV erosion in disguise.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Show how yield + capped upside translated into total return each calendar year, including down years where option premium did NOT offset the underlying loss. Quote per-share distribution year-by-year, the latest-year ROC share, and the divergence between total return and price-only return — a flat-to-positive total return on top of a steadily declining NAV is structural NAV erosion (QYLD is the textbook case). Worst calendar year vs both the equity benchmark AND a high-dividend equity ETF.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Smooth-ride delivery is the core mandate. Quote calendar-year hit rate, the worst single year, and the same-period 100% S&P 500 worst year side-by-side — a moderate-allocation fund should have a materially smaller worst year than pure equity. For target-date glide paths, compare to the issuer's own glide-path target. The bond-sleeve distribution should track its yield; flag any abrupt drops.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Quote calendar-year hit rate and the worst calendar year. 2022 was the rate-shock test — long IG / government / muni lost -25% to -31%, intermediate core ~-13%, ultrashort / MMF ~+1%. Compare the worst year to a duration-matched Treasury index. Distribution should closely track SEC yield; large gaps signal smoothing or de-accumulated coupons.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: Quote calendar-year hit rate and worst single year. 2020 COVID: HY -15-20%, bank loans -5-10%, EM debt -15%, preferreds -15-25%. 2022 hit longer-duration credit harder. Show per-share distribution year-by-year, especially around 2020 / 2022. Break out the ROC share for preferreds and convertibles where it is structurally larger. Compare the worst year to the matching credit benchmark.

factors.factorDescription: Looks at three things together — the fund's AUM trajectory over time, current daily dollar volume, and bid-ask spread — i.e. 'can retail trade this fund cheaply and confidently?'. AUM trend: rising AUM signals retail and institutional adoption; sustained outflows signal lost confidence in the strategy or asset class; below ~$50M with flat-or-declining trend is real closure / merger risk (issuer may liquidate, merge into a sibling fund, or stop marketing). Daily dollar volume threshold: comfortably above $1M / day supports normal retail lot sizes without visible slippage; thin volume forces wide limit orders and partial fills. Bid-ask spread bands: under 5 bps is tight (mega broad-equity funds), 5-15 bps is typical (sector / thematic, IG credit), 15-30 bps is normal (muni, EM debt, niche alt, futures-based commodity), above 30 bps is a real round-trip cost that taxes every entry and exit. Very large AUM in narrow / illiquid strategies (single-country EM, small-cap niche, capacity-constrained thematics) can hit capacity limits and hurt forward returns. Use current AUM and bid-ask from the data; for AUM trend, look up historical AUM (1Y / 3Y / 5Y / since inception) from the issuer fact sheet, etf.com, or other reputable public sources via the Factor-metric lookup rule. Strong recent return tends to correlate with strong inflows (and vice versa), so AUM trend doubles as a market-validated read on the last year's performance. Pass when AUM is at or above category-typical levels AND trend is flat-to-rising AND daily dollar volume + bid-ask support retail-sized round-trips. Fail when AUM is well below the survival threshold for the category, OR has dropped 50%+ from peak without an asset-class decline of similar magnitude, OR bid-ask is consistently wide enough to materially tax retail round-trips, OR daily dollar volume is too thin for retail-sized orders.
factors.factorMetrics: aum, sharesOut, volume, avgVolume, dollarVol, marketBidAskSpread, inceptionDate, return1y

groupInstructions.broad-equity: For mega broad-market funds (SPY ~$500B+, VTI, IVV), AUM trend matters mainly for fee competitiveness and liquidity depth — small relative moves are not decision-changing. For newer or niche broad-equity funds (factor tilts, themed dividend ETFs, foreign / single-region funds), sustained outflows signal lost confidence in the tilt. Below $100M for newer funds = closure risk worth flagging. Bid-ask: mega broad-equity funds run sub-5 bps; smaller niche or single-region funds typically 10-25 bps.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: AUM in thematic ETFs is highly cyclical — money piles in at theme peaks (clean energy 2020-21, robotics 2018-21, AI 2023-24) and out at troughs; outflows of 60-80% from peak are common after a theme falls out of favor. What matters most for retail is closure risk (below ~$50M) vs simply out-of-favor. Single-country EM funds also need enough AUM for trading liquidity. Bid-ask: 5-15 bps for mega sector funds (XLK, XLF, XLE); 15-30 bps for niche thematics; widens fast on thin AUM.
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Leveraged products see fast AUM swings tied to retail sentiment and short-term performance — TQQQ flows mirror Nasdaq sentiment, SQQQ picks up in bear markets. Below $50M, closure / merger risk is real (multiple leveraged products have been delisted). High AUM in a small-cap or narrow-sector leveraged product can hurt daily-tracking quality due to derivative-position limits. Bid-ask: 5-15 bps for liquid majors (TQQQ, SQQQ, UPRO, SPXU); 20-50+ bps for narrow-sector or low-AUM leveraged products.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Commodity / crypto wrappers see AUM swings with the underlying — GLD AUM tracks gold sentiment; spot-BTC ETFs (IBIT, FBTC) tracked BTC adoption post-spot-ETF approval. For futures-based commodity funds, sustained AUM signals that roll cost has been absorbable; shrinking AUM in a roll-cost-burdened fund is a closure warning. For single-commodity wrappers (oil, natural gas), thin AUM amplifies tracking issues. Bid-ask: 5-15 bps for major commodity wrappers (GLD, IAU, SLV); 15-30 bps for single-commodity futures (USO, UNG); 5-20 bps for spot crypto leaders (IBIT, FBTC); 30-60+ bps for thin or new wrappers.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: AUM growth has been a defining story for derivative-income funds in the 2020s — JEPI grew from $20M to $30B+, QYLD has run at $5-10B, SPYI at $5B+. Sustained AUM growth signals retail income demand. Sudden outflows can signal the strategy stopped delivering or NAV erosion finally caught retail attention — read AUM trend alongside the NAV / distribution trajectory. Bid-ask: 5-15 bps for major derivative-income funds (JEPI, QYLD, SPYI); wider on newer niche options strategies.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Target-date funds get most of their AUM from 401(k) defaults — flows are mostly mechanical and sticky. Watch for unusual outflows that may signal advisor / plan-sponsor migration to a different family (common for actively-managed target-date series losing share to cheaper passive). For tactical / global allocation, AUM trend is the retail vote on the strategy. Bid-ask: ETF-format allocation funds typically 10-30 bps; mutual-fund-format target-date in 401(k) channels trades at NAV so spread is not a retail concern.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Bond-fund AUM moves with rate-cycle sentiment — IG core / Treasury flows surged 2023-2024 as rates peaked; muni flows are tied to tax-bracket investor demand. For ultrashort / MMF, AUM is sticky cash-substitute money that grows late-cycle and bleeds when rates fall. Below $100M, even bond funds face closure risk. Bid-ask: 1-5 bps for mega Treasury / IG core funds (BND, AGG, IEF, SHY); 10-25 bps for national muni; 20-40 bps for single-state muni and longer-duration TIPS.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: Credit-fund AUM is more volatile — HY / bank-loan / EM-debt flows surge in risk-on periods and bleed in risk-off; preferred / convertible flows mirror equity sentiment. AUM trend tells you whether retail / institutional money is currently being rewarded for taking the credit risk. Below $50M = closure risk. Bid-ask: 5-15 bps for HY mega funds (HYG, JNK); 15-30 bps for preferreds / convertibles / EM debt; 20-50 bps for bank loans and CLO funds. Stress-window blowouts (HYG -4% to NAV in March 2020) are normal for the asset class — note as a warning, not as a Fail.

factors.factorDescription: Looks at where the fund sits inside its category peer group across 1Y / 3Y / 5Y / 10Y. Use percentile and quartile rank: top quartile is above-average, second quartile near-average-but-better, third quartile below-average-but-acceptable, bottom quartile materially weak. Always report peer-group size alongside the rank — median in a 600-fund category is different from median in a 10-fund category. Track whether standing is improving, stable, or deteriorating and cite the actual percentile movement. For a passive index fund inside an active-heavy peer category, structural tracking-cost headwind means median-among-active is a Pass-grade outcome — do not Fail a passive fund on peer rank alone. Pass when the fund is in the top two quartiles over the longest available window AND the percentile trend is not deteriorating sharply. Fail when it sits in the bottom quartile across multiple windows without a mandate-based reason (e.g. a long-vol fund in the bottom quartile of a multi-strategy category during an equity bull is mandate-aligned, not a Fail).
factors.factorMetrics: percentileRanks, quartileRanks, numberOfInvestmentsInCategory, overviewCategory, returnVsCategory, riskVsCategory

groupInstructions.broad-equity: Large Blend, Large Value, Large Growth, Mid / Small Blend, Foreign Large Blend, Global Large-Stock Blend etc. are separate categories — compare only to peers in the SAME category. For passive index funds in an active-heavy peer category, median is a Pass-grade outcome because active managers carry a structural fee + tracking-cost headwind.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Sector categories are tight (Technology, Health, Real Estate, Utilities, Communications); thematic and miscellaneous-sector categories are more dispersed (Miscellaneous Sector, Theme, Equity Digital Assets). Single-country EM categories have small peer counts (India Equity, China Region, Latin America Stock) — report the count alongside the rank.
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Trading-Leveraged Equity, Trading-Inverse Equity, Trading-Leveraged Debt etc. are small peer categories. Rank between products inside a leverage bucket is mostly about daily-tracking quality and issuer execution — structural decay applies to every product in the category, so do not Fail a fund on rank alone if the decay is in line with peers.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Categories are small (Commodities Broad Basket, Commodities Precious Metals, Digital Assets, Single Currency) — report peer count. Category dispersion is wide; rank reads with that caveat. Physical-backed vs futures-based wrappers within the same category have different cost / tracking profiles — note which sub-group of peers the rank actually compares against.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Derivative Income, Defined Outcome, Equity Hedged, Multistrategy, Systematic Trend, Long-Short Equity, Market Neutral, Macro Trading, Multialternative, Event Driven, Relative Value Arbitrage, Volatility, Downside Hedge are separate categories — compare to the right one. Inside Derivative Income especially, peer dispersion is wide (JEPI vs QYLD vs SPYI have different option mechanics and underlying indices).
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Each target-date vintage is its own peer group (Target-Date 2030, 2040, 2050, 2055, 2060, 2065+, Target-Date Retirement); allocation buckets are split too (Conservative / Moderate / Aggressive / Moderately Conservative / Moderately Aggressive Allocation, Tactical Allocation, Global Allocation variants, Target Risk, Target Outcome). Stay inside the specific category.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Bond categories are duration-defined (Ultrashort Bond, Short-Term Bond, Intermediate Core Bond, Intermediate Core-Plus, Long-Term Bond, Short / Intermediate / Long Government, Inflation-Protected Bond, Short-Term Inflation-Protected). Muni categories split by national vs single-state and by maturity (Muni National Short / Interm / Long, Muni California / NY / NJ / MA / OH / MN). Compare within the exact category.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: High Yield Bond, Bank Loan, Preferred Stock, Convertibles, Emerging Markets Bond, Emerging-Markets Local-Currency Bond, Multisector Bond, Nontraditional Bond, High Yield Muni, Securitized Bond-Focused, Broad Credit, Private Debt-General — each is its own category. Compare within the specific one.
